original_hypothesis.title: L2 Target Dispenser Batch Processing DoS via Malicious Staking Contract deposit() Revert
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData(), the verification of staking targets uses a low-level call (which handles failures gracefully), but the subsequent IStaking(target).deposit(amount) is a regular Solidity call. If a valid-looking staking target's deposit() function reverts, the entire _processData transaction reverts, blocking ALL other targets in the same bridge message batch from receiving their OLAS incentives.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The StakingFactory's permissionless createStakingInstance allows anyone to deploy staking instances. A malicious instance could pass all verifier checks but have a deposit() that conditionally reverts. Since _processData processes all targets in a single transaction without try/catch around deposit(), one bad target blocks the entire batch.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Low-level call for verification (line 147-148) vs regular call for deposit (line 165); deposit revert propagates to entire function

preconditions: Attacker deploys a staking instance via StakingFactory that passes verifier checks
Attacker gets the instance voted for in VoteWeighting (requires veOLAS)
The instance's deposit() function conditionally reverts
Bridge message batch includes both the malicious and legitimate targets

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: IToken(olas).approve(target, amount); IStaking(target).deposit(amount); — no try/catch, revert propagates
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A single reverting deposit() blocks all targets in the batch. Factory verification at lines 147-153 uses low-level call but deposit at line 165 does not.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around deposit(), no per-target error handling

analysis_questions: Can a staking contract pass all verifier checks while having a conditionally reverting deposit()?
How often are multiple targets batched in a single bridge message?
What is the recovery path via processDataMaintenance?
validation_requirements: Create a staking contract that passes StakingVerifier checks but reverts on deposit()
Demonstrate batch DoS by including it in a multi-target bridge message
Verify that legitimate targets are permanently blocked until DAO intervention

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys malicious staking instance through factory, gets votes directed to it, bridge message batches it with legitimate targets, deposit() reverts blocking entire batch, requires DAO to call processDataMaintenance excluding malicious target.
prerequisites: Ability to deploy staking instance via factory

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary denial of staking incentives for all L2 targets on the affected chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All staking programs on the affected L2 chain
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Recoverable via DAO processDataMaintenance call, but delayed

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for deposit() revert handling in batch processing

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Triggered when bridge message is delivered to L2
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Bridge message delivery failure visible on-chain

validation_result.mechanism: Missing try/catch around deposit() in batch processing loop
validation_result.impact_summary: A single reverting IStaking(target).deposit(amount) call in DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData() reverts the entire batch, blocking all legitimate staking targets in the same bridge message from receiving OLAS incentives.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The _processData() function in DefaultTargetDispenserL2.sol uses a deliberate low-level call for staking factory verification (with the comment 'This is a low level call since it must never revert'), but then uses a regular high-level Solidity call for IStaking(target).deposit(amount). This inconsistency means any revert in deposit() propagates through the entire _processData execution, blocking all remaining targets in the same batch.

The attack vector is viable because: (1) StakingFactory.createStakingInstance() is permissionless, (2) if implementationsCheck is false on the verifier (or the verifier is zero address on the factory), any implementation can be used including a malicious one with a conditionally reverting deposit(), (3) even with implementationsCheck true, legitimate implementations' deposit() can revert under certain conditions (e.g., token transfer issues, state changes between verification and deposit). The malicious target would pass the low-level verification call (returning non-zero emissions amount) but then revert on the subsequent deposit() call.

The recovery path requires DAO intervention via processDataMaintenance() (which calls the same _processData but with crafted data excluding the problematic target), introducing delays in incentive distribution.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code pattern is clearly visible in DefaultTargetDispenserL2._processData(): the verification at stakingFactory.call(verifyData) is a low-level call that catches failures, while IStaking(target).deposit(amount) is a regular Solidity call that propagates reverts. The comment 'This is a low level call since it must never revert' on the verification call confirms the developers were aware of the need for graceful failure handling but did not extend the same protection to the deposit call. The same pattern exists in EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit() and DefaultTargetDispenserL2.redeem().

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The verification uses a low-level call to prevent reverts, but the deposit uses a regular Solidity call. If deposit() reverts for any target, the entire _processData transaction fails, blocking all other targets in the batch.

Same pattern on L1: verification uses a regular call that reverts on zero (blocking the batch), and deposit() has no try/catch. A single invalid target or reverting deposit blocks all targets in the batch.
The redeem function also uses a regular deposit call with no try/catch, though this processes one target at a time so the batch DoS impact is limited here.

calling_sequence: Bridge delivers message to L2 TargetDispenser (e.g., GnosisTargetDispenserL2.receiveMessage or onTokenBridged)
_processData decodes targets[] and amounts[] from bridge message
For each target: low-level call to stakingFactory.verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount (graceful failure)
If verification passes (limitAmount > 0) and balance sufficient: IToken(olas).approve(target, amount)
IStaking(target).deposit(amount) — regular Solidity call, revert propagates
If malicious target's deposit() reverts → entire _processData reverts → no targets processed
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: _receiveMessage → _processData → loop over targets → stakingFactory.call (low-level, catches failures) → IStaking(target).deposit(amount) (high-level, propagates revert). When the deposit call reverts for any single target, the entire transaction reverts, and the state change for stakingBatchNonce and all other targets' deposits are rolled back. The bridge message is consumed but the funds remain unprocessed on L2.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Attack scenario validation:
1. Attacker deploys a staking instance via StakingFactory using a malicious implementation (possible if implementationsCheck is false on StakingVerifier, or verifier is zero address). The malicious deposit() conditionally reverts (e.g., always reverts, or reverts after first call).
2. Attacker needs veOLAS to vote for the malicious instance via VoteWeighting. This is a capital requirement but not prohibitive.
3. The malicious instance passes verifyInstanceAndGetEmissionsAmount (returns non-zero) because it's registered in the factory with isEnabled=true.
4. When bridge message arrives containing both the malicious target and legitimate targets in the same batch, _processData processes them in a single transaction.
5. The low-level verification call succeeds for the malicious target.
6. IStaking(maliciousTarget).deposit(amount) reverts.
7. The entire transaction reverts, blocking ALL targets in the batch.

Even without a malicious implementation, any state condition in a legitimate staking contract that causes deposit() to revert (token issues, overflow, etc.) would trigger the same DoS.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact: All staking targets on the affected L2 chain are blocked from receiving OLAS incentives until the DAO intervenes via processDataMaintenance(). The bridge message tokens remain on the L2 dispenser but are not distributed. Recovery requires DAO governance action to call processDataMaintenance with data excluding the problematic target, which introduces delays (governance voting period). During this time, all staking programs on the affected chain are denied their incentives.
recommended_mitigations: Wrap IStaking(target).deposit(amount) in a try/catch block within _processData(). On catch, queue the deposit for later redeem (using the existing stakingQueueingNonces mechanism) instead of reverting the entire batch.
Apply the same try/catch pattern to IToken(olas).approve(target, amount) since approve can also theoretically revert.
In EthereumDepositProcessor._deposit(), replace the hard revert on limitAmount == 0 with the same withholding mechanism used on L2, and add try/catch around deposit().
Consider adding a per-target gas limit for deposit calls to prevent gas griefing attacks.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a malicious StakingToken-like contract with deposit() that reverts conditionally (e.g., always reverts or reverts after N calls). 2. Deploy it via StakingFactory (with verifier disabled or implementation whitelisted). 3. Set up a DefaultTargetDispenserL2 test with two targets: one legitimate, one malicious. 4. Craft bridge message data with both targets. 5. Call _processData (or simulate bridge message delivery). 6. Verify the entire transaction reverts and the legitimate target receives no funds. 7. Verify processDataMaintenance can recover by excluding the malicious target.
uncertainties: Whether implementationsCheck is enabled in production deployments — if enabled, the attack requires finding a way to make a whitelisted implementation's deposit() revert rather than deploying a custom malicious implementation
Whether bridge implementations guarantee single-target delivery or always batch multiple targets — if bridges typically send one target per message, the batch DoS vector is reduced
The exact gas cost and governance delay for DAO to execute processDataMaintenance recovery
additional_testing_needed: Test with a legitimate StakingToken implementation where deposit() reverts due to edge cases (e.g., token with blacklisting, token with transfer hooks)
Test batch processing with mixed valid/invalid targets to confirm entire batch fails
Test processDataMaintenance recovery flow with crafted data excluding problematic targets
Test whether the approve() call before deposit() can also be a revert vector
Verify the production deployment configuration of StakingVerifier.implementationsCheck
